title: What is a kWh, and why it is on your energy bill
description: A kWh is the unit your energy bill charges you for. What one kilowatt hour is, what it costs in 2026, and how to work out any appliance's running cost.
blurb: The unit your whole bill is built on: what a kilowatt hour actually is, what it costs, and how to use it.
lede: Every energy bill in Britain is priced in kilowatt hours, yet most people could not say what one actually is. That is a shame, because the kWh is the single most useful number in home energy. Once you know what it represents and what your supplier charges for one, you can price up any appliance in your house in seconds, sanity-check your bill, compare tariffs properly, and spot which habits cost real money and which ones barely register. This guide explains the unit from scratch, with the current prices attached.
short_answer: A kilowatt hour (kWh) is the energy used by a 1,000 watt appliance running for one hour. It is the unit your supplier counts on your meter and multiplies by your unit rate to build your bill. Under the Ofgem price cap from July to September 2026, one kWh of electricity costs 26.11p on average and one kWh of gas costs 7.33p. A typical GB home uses around 2,700 kWh of electricity and 11,500 kWh of gas a year.

sections.h2: A kWh measures energy, not power
sections.html:
The confusion usually starts with two similar-looking units. A kilowatt (kW) measures power, which is how fast something uses energy at a given moment. A kilowatt hour (kWh) measures energy, which is the total amount used over time. Power is the speed; energy is the distance travelled.

The relationship is simple multiplication: energy in kWh equals power in kW times the hours it runs. A 1 kW appliance running for one hour uses exactly 1 kWh. So does a 2 kW appliance running for half an hour, or a 100 W appliance running for ten hours. Same energy, same cost, delivered at different speeds.

A few real examples make it concrete:

  • A 3 kW kettle uses 1 kWh after about 20 minutes of total boiling time.
  • A 2 kW fan heater uses 1 kWh every 30 minutes it runs flat out.
  • A 10 W LED bulb takes 100 hours, roughly two weeks of evening use, to get through 1 kWh.

This is why high-power heating appliances dominate most bills while lights and phone chargers barely matter. The wattage on the label tells you how quickly the meter will spin, and the hours of use do the rest.

sections.h2: Where the kWh appears on your bill
sections.html:
Open any energy bill and you will find two charges per fuel. The first is the unit rate: a price per kWh multiplied by the number of kWh you used in the billing period. The second is the standing charge, a fixed daily fee you pay regardless of usage. We cover the fixed part separately in our guide to standing charges; everything else on the bill flows from kWh times unit rate.

Under the Ofgem price cap for July to September 2026, the average direct debit rates in Great Britain are:

  • Electricity: 26.11p per kWh, plus a 57.19p daily standing charge.
  • Gas: 7.33p per kWh, plus a 29.04p daily standing charge.

Your exact rates vary by region and payment method, so check your own bill or your supplier's app for the figures that apply to you. Our walkthrough of how to read your energy bill shows where each number lives.

One quirk worth knowing: your electricity meter counts kWh directly, but your gas meter measures volume in cubic metres. The bill converts that volume to kWh using a fixed formula (units used, times a volume correction factor of 1.02264, times the calorific value of the gas, divided by 3.6). You never need to do this sum yourself, but it explains why the units on your gas meter do not match the kWh on the bill. If you are taking your own readings, our guide to reading your energy meter covers both meter types.

sections.h2: What one kWh actually gets you
sections.html:
The easiest way to build an instinct for the unit is to see how long different appliances take to use one. Every row in this table costs the same 26.11p; only the time changes.

ApplianceTypical powerHow long 1 kWh lasts
Electric shower9,000 WAbout 7 minutes (one quick shower)
Kettle3,000 W20 minutes (roughly nine 1 litre boils)
Electric oven2,000 W30 minutes
Fan heater2,000 W30 minutes on full power
Washing machine, 30°C cycleAbout 0.5 kWh per cycleTwo full loads
55 inch LED TV100 W10 hours
Laptop50 W20 hours
Fridge freezerAbout 30 W averaged outAround a day and a half
LED bulb10 W100 hours

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Appliance powers are typical values; check the label or manual for yours.

The pattern is clear. Anything that makes heat with electricity (showers, kettles, ovens, heaters, tumble dryers) burns through kWh quickly. Electronics and lighting sip them. A whole evening of television costs about 2.6p, while ten extra minutes in an electric shower costs around 39p. If you want to cut a bill, start with the heat.

sections.h2: How to work out any appliance's running cost
sections.html:
Once you know the unit, every running cost question in this site reduces to one formula:

Cost = (watts ÷ 1,000) × hours used × your unit rate in pence

Worked example: a 2,000 W electric heater running for three hours uses 2 kW × 3 hours = 6 kWh. At 26.11p per kWh that is 156.66p, so about £1.57 for the evening. Run it like that every night through a 90 day winter and it adds up to roughly £141.

Three practical tips when you apply it:

  • Use real watts, not peak watts. The label shows the maximum draw. Thermostatic appliances such as ovens, fridges and many heaters cycle on and off, so their average consumption is lower than the label suggests. A plug-in energy monitor or a smart meter's in-home display will show you actual figures.
  • Mind the units. Divide watts by 1,000 to get kilowatts before multiplying. A 100 W television is 0.1 kW, not 100 kW. This slip is behind most scary-looking but wrong running cost claims.
  • Use your own unit rate. The cap figure is a GB average. Your bill may say 24p or 28p depending on region and tariff, and on a time-of-use tariff the rate changes by the hour.

We keep a fuller version of this method, with worked examples for dozens of household items, in our guide to working out any appliance's running cost.

sections.h2: Why a gas kWh is cheaper than an electric one, and why that is not the whole story
sections.html:
A kWh of gas and a kWh of electricity contain exactly the same amount of energy, but they are priced very differently. At current cap rates, gas costs 7.33p per kWh and electricity 26.11p, so electricity is about three and a half times dearer per unit. That gap is why heating a home with plug-in electric heaters costs so much more than running a gas boiler, even though the heaters themselves are 100% efficient at the point of use.

The comparison has more moving parts than the headline rates, though:

  • Efficiency differs. A modern condensing boiler turns roughly 85 to 90% of each gas kWh into useful heat, so a kWh of warmth from gas really costs around 8 to 9p. An electric heater delivers all of its kWh as heat, at 26.11p.
  • Heat pumps change the maths. A heat pump moves heat rather than making it, delivering around three kWh of warmth for each kWh of electricity it consumes. That brings the cost per kWh of heat down to roughly 9p, into the same territory as gas.
  • Not all electric kWh cost the same. Off-peak tariffs such as Economy 7 sell overnight electricity at a discount, and EV tariffs go further still, with off-peak rates around 7 to 9p per kWh in mid 2026. The kWh is the same unit; the clock decides the price.

For a typical household on the current cap, the annual sums look like this: 2,700 kWh of electricity costs about £705 in unit charges plus £209 in standing charges, and 11,500 kWh of gas costs about £843 plus £106. Most of a dual fuel bill is gas kWh, cheap per unit but used in bulk for heating and hot water.

A kWh is just a fixed parcel of energy: one kilowatt of power sustained for one hour. Your meter counts these parcels, your supplier prices them, and everything on your bill beyond the standing charge is that count times that price. At the July to September 2026 cap, the parcel costs 26.11p for electricity and 7.33p for gas.

The practical payoff is the formula. Watts divided by 1,000, times hours, times your unit rate gives you the cost of running anything in your home. Apply it a few times and you will stop worrying about chargers and bulbs, and start noticing the electric shower, the tumble dryer and the portable heater, because those are the appliances that move the bill. From there, the rest of this site is just that one sum applied room by room.

faq.q: Is a kWh the same as a unit of electricity?
faq.a: Yes. When a bill, a meter or a supplier refers to a unit, it means one kilowatt hour. The two terms are interchangeable, so a unit rate of 26.11p means 26.11p per kWh.

faq.q: How much does one kWh cost in the UK right now?
faq.a: Under the Ofgem price cap from July to September 2026, the GB direct debit average is 26.11p per kWh for electricity and 7.33p per kWh for gas. Your exact rate depends on your region, tariff and payment method, so check your bill, and check Ofgem's website for the current cap once this period ends.

faq.q: What is the difference between a kW and a kWh?
faq.a: A kW measures power, the rate at which energy is being used at a moment in time. A kWh measures energy, the total used over time. A 2 kW heater running for one hour uses 2 kWh; running for 30 minutes it uses 1 kWh.

faq.q: Why does my gas meter not show kWh?
faq.a: Gas meters measure the volume of gas in cubic metres (or hundreds of cubic feet on older meters). Your supplier converts that volume to kWh on the bill using a standard formula involving a correction factor and the calorific value of the gas, because the energy content of gas varies slightly.

faq.q: How many kWh does a typical UK home use?
faq.a: Ofgem's typical domestic consumption values are around 2,700 kWh of electricity and 11,500 kWh of gas per year for a medium-use household. A small flat may use far less; a large home with electric heating or an EV can use several times more electricity.
